headline: Against the Odds: Australian Family Welcomes Rare Identical Quadruplets
meta_description: One in 15 million: An Australian woman safely delivered rare identical quadruplets sharing a single placenta after a high-risk pregnancy monitored closely by s…
summary_short: A Brisbane woman has given birth to naturally-conceived identical quadruplets—an occurrence doctors estimate happens in just one in 15 million pregnancies.
body:
When Jenitar Na'amoana and her husband Jortham learned they were expecting quadruplets, the news arrived as an "immense surprise"—especially since the couple already had four children between one and 10 years old.

Yet what made their story truly extraordinary wasn't just the number of babies coming, but the medical rarity of how they arrived. The four girls—Emily, Harriet, Catherine, and Alexa—are monozygotic quadruplets, meaning they developed from a single fertilised egg that split into four embryos. Even more remarkable: they shared the same placenta, a circumstance Dr Alexa Bendall, the maternal foetal medicine specialist who cared for Na'amoana, described as "unheard of."

Identical quadruplets occur in roughly one in every 15 million pregnancies. Naturally conceived quadruplets are even rarer—estimated at one in 700,000 births.

The pregnancy was classified as extremely high-risk. The Royal Brisbane and Women's Hospital admitted Na'amoana as an inpatient at 25 weeks for continuous monitoring. "We always said that if we could get her to 28 weeks, we would be doing well," Bendall recalled.

On 14 July, all four girls arrived via caesarean section at 28 weeks and four days—healthy and thriving in the neonatal intensive care unit. The grateful parents have since launched a fundraising campaign for a larger vehicle, which has already raised over A$37,000 in donations.
